retrieved_docs.title: Epoetin Alfa -- Indications -- Experimental Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Friedreich ataxia and frataxin levels: Erythropoietin increases frataxin levels in peripheral blood mononuclear cells, a key factor in Friedreich ataxia, characterized by low frataxin levels. In a 2016 randomized controlled trial of patients with Friedreich ataxia, epoetin alfa did not impact the primary endpoint of peak oxygen uptake or affect frataxin levels. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Erythropoietin Stimulating Agents -- Continuing Education Activity

retrieved_docs.title: Epoetin Alfa -- Indications -- Experimental Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Neuroprotection in preterm infants: Erythropoietin is hypothesized to have neuroprotective effects in preterm infants. A 2020 randomized controlled trial found that epoetin alfa was not associated with improved mortality or reduced severe neurodevelopmental impairment by the age of 2 years. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Ventral Cord Syndrome -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Current preclinical research is studying promising pharmacological agents as a possible treatment for ischemic spinal cord injuries. Neuroprotective therapies have as their basis the premise of limiting secondary injury after the initial damage by modulating inflammation, reducing cell death, and guarding against excitotoxicity. There are currently no proven pharmacological therapies for human spinal cord injury that provide neuroprotection. Studies use propofol, thymoquinone, minocycline, erythropoietin, and curcumin as possible neuroprotective pharmacologic agents. Antioxidants are used to protect against the oxidative damage caused by secondary neurodegeneration after acute SCI. Antioxidants seem to stabilize mitochondrial bioenergetics, protect neurons and achieve modest neurological recover after injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Spinal Artery Syndrome -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Current preclinical research is exploring promising pharmacological agents as a possible treatment for ischemic spinal cord injuries. Neuroprotective therapies limit secondary injury after the initial damage by modulating inflammation, reducing cell death, and guarding against excitotoxicity. Currently, no proven pharmacological therapies for human spinal cord injury provide neuroprotection. Studies use propofol, thymoquinone, minocycline, erythropoietin, and curcumin as possible neuroprotective pharmacologic agents. Antioxidants protect against the oxidative damage caused by secondary neurodegeneration after acute spinal cord injury. These antioxidants also stabilize mitochondrial bioenergetics, protect neurons, and achieve modest neurological recovery.

retrieved_docs.title: Tofersen -- Mechanism of Action -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Mutated SOD1 protein is known to have toxic effects on motor neurons and astrocytes, the major cell types involved in ALS development and progression. [13] By lowering the levels of SOD1 protein in the CSF and blood, tofersen can slow down the rate of disease progression and enhance the quality of life of patients with SOD1 ALS.

retrieved_docs.content: Formal care at the end of life should incorporate measures to improve the quality of life (such as a system to enable effective communication) and focus upon the management of commonly encountered symptoms. The use of opioids and benzodiazepines for breathlessness, glycopyrrolate for increased secretions, antipsychotics for delirium, and other non-pharmacological treatment options may be indicated in patients approaching their end of life. retrieved_docs.content: Another consideration for MLS patients is to avoid heterologous blood product transfusions due to an increased risk of adverse transfusion reactions. Case reports have suggested avoiding alpha-tocopherol and idebenone in treating the neuromuscular symptoms of patients with PKAN due to anecdotal evidence suggesting worsening of symptoms. [6] Finally, patients should avoid iron supplementation or other sources of exogenous iron due to the iron accumulation inherent in the disease. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This topic will review the etiology, diagnosis, and evaluation of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). In addition, it outlines the treatments and interventions available. This topic highlights the role of the interprofessional healthcare team in evaluating and treating patients with ALS.

retrieved_docs.title: Trinucleotide Repeat Disorders -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Potential Non-Antioxidant Friedrich Ataxia Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Emerging modalities in this category include the following: Deferiprone is a small molecule that preferentially binds iron, preventing the accumulation of reactive oxygen species and thereby reducing oxidative stress. Erythropoietin plays a significant role in the brain's response to neuronal injury and contributes to the wound-healing process. Histone deacetylase inhibitors modulate the acetylation levels of chromosomal proteins and other cellular targets, converting silent heterochromatin into active chromatin to restore normal function to silenced genes. Interferon γ1b has been shown to increase frataxin expression in dorsal root ganglion neurons in treated mouse models, which also demonstrated improved sensorimotor performance.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Motor Neuron Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Because there is no cure for ALS and the prognosis is grave, clinicians must diagnose the disease with confirmation by NCS and needle EMG as soon as possible so that the appropriate interventions can be taken. There are only a few approved FDA treatments for ALS. Riluzole blocks glutamate release from neurons and has been shown to prolong survival by a few months. Riluzole can cause an elevation in liver enzymes; therefore, liver function testing should be ordered before administering this medication, as well as periodically. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Tofersen -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Screen potential ALS patients with SOD1 gene mutations and disease severity who may benefit from tofersen treatment. Apply best practices for pharmacovigilance to identify and manage any adverse events associated with tofersen use in patients to avoid treatment-related complications. Assess the response to tofersen treatment by regularly evaluating ALS symptoms, disease progression, and changes in quality of life following the therapy. Communicate effectively with patients and their caregivers about the potential benefits, risks, and expectations of tofersen therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1578
retrieved_docs.content: Alkylating agents, amphotericin B, antidepressants, antigout preparations, azathioprine, beta‑carotene, botulinum toxins, diethylstilbestrol, diltiazem, erythropoietin, conjugated estrogens (USP), glucose, the fixed‑dose combination of amlodipine and valsartan, nicotine, protective agents, hexachlorophene, paclitaxel, sodium tetradecyl sulfate, thalidomide, penicillins, permethrin, silymarin, spiro‑compounds (including spiro‑derived compounds) and hexachlorophen are all recognized as agents that can cause poisoning, underscoring the need for careful risk assessment in clinical practice. Despite this toxic potential, each of these substances carries an “allowed qualifier” for therapeutic use: alkylating agents serve as chemotherapeutic drugs; amphotericin B is an antifungal; antidepressants treat mood disorders; antigout preparations manage gout; azathioprine functions as an immunosuppressant; beta‑carotene is used as a nutritional supplement; botulinum toxins are employed in

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 831

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Amphotericin B, antidepressants, antigout preparations, β‑carotene, botulinum toxins, diethylstilbestrol, diltiazem, erythropoietin, conjugated estrogens (USP), glucose, the fixed‑dose combination of amlodipine and valsartan, nicotine, various protective agents, hexachlorophene, paclitaxel, penicillins, permethrin, silymarin, spiro compounds (spirosloučeniny), sodium tetradecyl sulfate and thalidomide are all documented in the medical literature as agents that can be involved in poisoning scenarios. These substances may act as toxins themselves or precipitate toxic reactions when misused, overdosed, or encountered inadvertently, and each carries an adverse‑effect profile that requires careful prescribing, monitoring, and patient counseling. Sodium tetradecyl sulfate and thalidomide are additionally recognized for therapeutic use, while spiro compounds are uniquely classified with dual qualifiers reflecting simultaneous therapeutic applications and potential to cause poisoning. Hexachlorophene, sodium tetradecyl sulfate and thalidomide are specifically highlighted as toxic substances whose exposure can produce harmful health effects, necessitating vigilant management in both clinical and environmental contexts. Awareness of both the beneficial and harmful properties of these agents is essential for clinicians to prevent toxic exposures, recognize poisoning early, implement appropriate antidotal or supportive therapy, and balance therapeutic benefits against possible risks.
